steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Social buffering means the presence of a companion reduces a rattlesnake's stress response. The researchers measured stress using percent change between baseline and peak heart rate — higher values mean more stress. A supporting finding must show that snakes with a companion had a lower percent change than those without one.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Average peak heart rates were highest among solitary C. helleri, but no differences were observed in average peak heart rates between C. helleri with a companion and C. helleri with a rope.: Uses absolute peak heart rate, not percent change — the wrong metric. Also shows no difference between companion and rope conditions. ✗
  • Choice B — C. helleri with a companion displayed a lower average baseline heart rate and lower average peak heart rate than did solitary C. helleri or C. helleri with a rope.: Again uses absolute heart rate values, not percent change. A lower baseline and lower peak could still produce the same percent change. ✗
  • Choice C — The average percent change in heart rate was lower among C. helleri with a companion than among solitary C. helleri and C. helleri with a rope.: Directly uses percent change — the correct metric — and shows companions reduced it compared to both other conditions. This is exactly what social buffering predicts. ✓
  • Choice D — Solitary C. helleri had higher average baseline heart rates than did C. helleri in the other treatments, but the average percent change in heart rate was smaller among solitary C. helleri than among C. helleri with a companion.: Shows solitary snakes had a smaller percent change — the opposite of what social buffering predicts. ✗
That matches Choice C — The average percent change in heart rate was lower among C. helleri with a companion than among solitary C. helleri and C. helleri with a rope..
